Section

  • HNSW
  • IVF
  • Quantization

Vector Search Index Tuning

Choosing an index is a tradeoff between recall, latency, and memory. The three families you will encounter in practice are flat (brute force), HNSW (graph-based), and IVF (inverted-file based).

HNSW

Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs maintain layered proximity links. The two tuning knobs that matter are M (graph fan-out) and ef_construction / ef_search (candidate list size). Higher values increase recall and memory; lower values reduce both. Start with M=16, ef_construction=200, then sweep ef_search from 32 upward until recall@10 crosses the threshold you need.

IVF

Inverted-file indexes cluster vectors into Voronoi cells using k-means. Queries probe the nearest nprobe cells. IVF scales to billions of vectors when paired with product quantization, but at small N (under 1M) HNSW usually dominates on both speed and recall. The rule of thumb: pick nlist ≈ sqrt(N) and nprobe ≈ 1–5% of nlist.

Quantization

Scalar quantization (int8) cuts memory by 4x with minor recall loss. Product quantization is more aggressive — 32x compression is typical — but only worth the complexity above tens of millions of vectors. Binary quantization (1-bit per dimension) is the extreme; pair it with rerank passes against the full vectors.

What to measure

Track recall@10 against a ground-truth flat index on a held-out query set. Track p99 latency under your real query distribution, not just synthetic load. Memory usage matters when you scale horizontally — a cheaper-per-host index lets you fit more shards per node.

Example RAG docs.
